content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 23, 1993

Burt McChesney
California Association of Realtors
980 Ninth Street, Suite 1430
Sacramento, CA 95814-2734

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-421

Dear Mr. McChesney:

You have requested advice regarding the gift provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

As a registered lobbyist, is the entertainment by you and
your family of "hot list" guests aboard a sailboat a,reportable
gift?

CONCLUSION

Such entertainment would constitute a .reportable gift to
guests whom you and your family entertain aboard the boat.

FACTS

You are registered as a lobbyist employed by the California
Association of Realtors. You are a member of a sailing club,
based in Richmond, California, which offers a boat lease program
to the general public. Under the terms of the club's contract,
you have a renewable one year contract to pay a $240 monthly
rental fee which entitles you to the unlimited use of any boat in
the club's fleet, conditional on boat availability and your
demonstrated ability to safely operate the selected vessel.

The vessels you are currently authorized to use are all
sailboats ranging in size from 28 to 39 feet in length, which
qualify as residences under federal and state tax law. Your usage

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-421
Page 2

of the vessels is exactly like the use made of other types of
second homes. You, family and guests use the vessels as
residences during overnight or weekend-long visits to the bay
area. You often use the boats on a weekend basis to entertain
"hot list" guests, including members of the legislature and staff.

Neither your monthly fees nor any incidental expenses related
to the boat rental agreement (mileage, parking, tolls, key
deposits, etc.) are reimbursed by your employer.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a lobbyist or lobbying firm from making
gifts to one person aggregating more than ten dollars ($10) in a
calendar month (Section 86203). The Act defines a gift to include
any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater
value is not received. (Section 82028.) "Payment" includes the
rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value.
(Section 82044.) This would apply to the rendering of lodging.

Regulation 18630 provides a "home hospitality" exception to
the gift limitation applicable to lobbyists. It states that:

     (a) The cost of providing hospitality
involving food, beverage or occasional lodging at
the home of a lobbyist is a gift within the meaning
of Section 86203 and is reportable under the
provisions of Section 86113 only if:

          (1) Any part of the cost of such
     hospitality is paid for by the lobbyist's
     employer or lobbying firm directly; or

          (2) The lobbyist is reimbursed by his or
     her lobbyist employer or lobbying firm for any
     part of the cost of such hospitality; or

          (3) The lobbyist deducts any part of the
     cost of such hospitality as a business expense
     on any tax return, either State or Federal; or

          (4) There is an understanding between
     the lobbyist and his or her lobbyist employer
     or lobbying firm that the amount of
     compensation received by the lobbyist includes
     a portion to be utilized by the lobbyist to
     provide gifts of hospitality in the lobbyist's
     home.

     (b) In determining the applicability of
subsections (a)(1) through (a)(4) above, the cost
of providing hospitality does not include any part
of the value of rental of the home of the lobbyist,

File No. A-93-421
Page 3


nor does it include any depreciation on the
premises where the hospitality is extended.

(Emphasis added.)

Although you informed us that (a)(1) through (4) do not apply to
the hospitality you provide aboard the boat, this still leaves the
larger question of whether the boats qualify as "homes." We have
concluded they do not.

The exception in Section 18630 to the gift reporting rules
specifically states that the hospitality must occur in the home of
the individual. Express exceptions to the general rule are to be
strictly and narrowly construed and will not be extended beyond
the exceptions named. (San Diego Union v. City Council of the
City of San Diego (1983) 146 Cal.App.3d 947, 954.) Although this
exception could be interpreted to include "homes" other than a
primary residence, it is not broad enough to include entertainment
on the large sailboats that you describe.

Therefore, you are required to report gifts of hospitality to
"hot list" guests whom you and your family entertain aboard the
rental boat.

I trust this answers your question.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak
